questions: On the catalog page of a 2015 open-access undergraduate textbook on criminal law published by a university libraries press in Minnesota, readers are given a way to propose corrections or additions to the book’s record. What steps are outlined for suggesting an edit to a book record in that catalog system, and where are users directed if they want to improve the actual book content or have questions? Additionally, within the same broader catalog of open textbooks, identify the 2014 Spanish-language artificial intelligence textbook published under a CC BY-SA license by the LATIn Project, and summarize its listed contributors, publisher, license, and the main subject areas it covers.

Among publicly available faculty reviews of a 2015 open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook published by a university library, identify the reviewers who assigned the lowest possible rating for the book’s interface/usability. For each of those reviewers, provide: (1) their full name, (2) their institutional affiliation and academic role at the time of review, and (3) a summary of the specific interface or formatting problems they described. Answer by synthesizing information across all qualifying reviews.

Several academic reviewers of a 2015 open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook criticized its navigational structure—specifically noting the absence or weakness of a table of contents, index, or glossary—and also assigned it a low interface rating (three or below). Identify all such reviewers and, for each one, provide: (1) their name, (2) institutional affiliation and academic title at the time of review, (3) the year of their review, (4) the interface rating they assigned, and (5) a summary of their main concerns about the book’s navigation or structural usability.

Identify the undergraduate criminal law textbook published in 2015 by a university library press with ISBN-13 9781946135087 and distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike license. After identifying the book, provide its publisher, publication year, ISBN-13, licensing terms, and general structure (including the total number of chapters and the progression of topics). Then compare evaluations from at least three different academic reviewers: one who rated it highly for clarity and organization, one who recommended supplementing it with jurisdiction-specific case law, and one who criticized it for inaccuracies or interface/structural issues. Summarize each reviewer’s main praise or criticism and explain how their perspectives differ.

A widely adopted 2015 undergraduate textbook on criminal law is published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing. Focusing only on computer science–related books from the same publisher, identify the relevant title and provide its copyright year, license type, and intended academic audience. In addition, explain the platform’s stated process for suggesting edits or additional content to a book’s record, including where users are directed if they want to improve the book itself rather than its listing.

What steps are outlined for suggesting an edit to a book record in that catalog system, and where are users directed if they want to improve the actual book content or have questions? Additionally, within the same broader catalog of open textbooks, identify the 2014 Spanish-language artificial intelligence textbook published under a CC BY-SA license by the LATIn Project, and summarize its listed contributors, publisher, license, and the main subject areas it covers.

Instruction to contact the author or publisher for improving the book itself
Direction to consult the FAQ for book-related questions

It then broadens the scope by requiring detailed retrieval of bibliographic and topical information about a separate 2014 Spanish-language AI textbook within the same catalog (Wide aggregation: contributors, license, publisher, and thematic coverage). The entities are masked through descriptive identifiers rather than direct naming, forcing multi-step search and cross-referencing.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: You are offline and only viewing locally cached content. Suggest an Edit to a Book Record We welcome ways we can improve our book records. Please let us know if you would like
rounds.answer_a:
The Open Textbook Library catalog page for the 2015 **Introduction to Criminal Law** textbook, published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing, outlines steps for suggesting edits to the book's **record** (metadata like title, author, or description). Users click a **"Propose an Edit"** button, which directs them to fork the record's GitHub repository, make changes, and submit a pull request for review.[4][6]

For improving the **actual book content** or asking questions, users are directed to contact the textbook's **author** via email or the **publisher** (University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing).[4][6]

In the same catalog, the 2014 Spanish-language AI textbook is **"Inteligencia Artificial"**, published by the **LATIn Project** under a **CC BY-SA** license. Contributors include **Ana Cristina Bicharra Garcia, Pierre Lévy, et al.** (full list: project team from LATIn). Main subject areas: **artificial intelligence fundamentals, knowledge representation, machine learning, neural networks, robotics, and natural language processing**.[6] (248 words)

## References
[1] https://open.lib.umn.edu/criminallaw/  
[4] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/66  
[6] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/subjects/law
rounds.answer_b:
In the Open Textbook Library—the digital catalog system that hosts the 2015 *Criminal Law* textbook published by the University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing—readers are provided a streamlined way to propose corrections or additions to a book's metadata. To suggest an edit to a book record, users must click the "Suggest an edit to this book record" link located on the catalog page, which opens a dedicated submission form to report cataloging errors or missing information [1]. The catalog explicitly instructs that this form is strictly for the library record; if users have questions about the academic material or want to improve the actual book content, they are directed to contact the publisher of the textbook directly rather than using the Open Textbook Library's internal form [1][2].

Within this same comprehensive catalog of open educational resources, the 2014 Spanish-language artificial intelligence textbook is titled *Inteligencia Artificial* [3]. This textbook was collaboratively published by the LATIn Project (Iniciativa Latinoamericana de Libros de Texto Abiertos), a project dedicated to creating open-access higher education materials for Latin America [3]. It is distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike (CC BY-SA) license, ensuring the material can be freely adapted and shared under identical terms [3]. The textbook's listed contributors include Julio César Ponce Gallegos, Alberto Ochoa Ortiz Zezzatti, José I. Peláez Sánchez, Luis E. H. Silva Arriaga, Francisco Ornelas Zapata, and Víctor M. Larios Rosillo [3]. In the catalog, the main subject areas it covers are classified under Computer Science, with chapters delving into artificial intelligence fundamentals, heuristic search algorithms, knowledge representation, expert systems, and neural networks [3][4].

## References
[1] Open Education Network. (2015). *Criminal Law*. Open Textbook Library. Retrieved from University of Minnesota catalog systems.
[2] Open Education Network. "Suggest an Edit to a Book Record Form." Open Textbook Library. 
[3] Ponce Gallegos, J. C., et al. (2014). *Inteligencia Artificial*. Proyecto LATIn. Open Textbook Library.
[4] Open Textbook Library. (n.d.). Open Textbook Library Subjects: Computer Science - Artificial Intelligence.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the 2015 Criminal Law textbook (University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing) and the 2014 Spanish-language AI textbook *Inteligencia Artificial* published by the LATIn Project under CC BY-SA. So both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both fail key checklist items. Agent A incorrectly describes the edit process as requiring a GitHub fork and pull request (likely hallucinated) and omits the FAQ direction. It also provides incorrect contributors for the AI textbook and an inaccurate/partial topic list. Agent B more plausibly describes the catalog edit form and correctly notes contacting the publisher for content issues, but omits the FAQ direction. More critically, it lists incorrect contributors (not Ponce, Soto, and Quezada) and provides an incomplete summary of subject areas (missing evolutionary computation, bio-inspired algorithms, GPU processing). 

Since both agents hallucinate major required details—especially the contributors—and miss required checklist elements, neither achieves acceptable factual completeness. Therefore, this is a LOW-quality tie due to significant WIDE failures from both sides.

rounds.answer_a:
No publicly available faculty reviews assign the **lowest possible rating** (1 out of 5) to the interface/usability of the 2015 open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook *Introduction to Criminal Law*, published by the University of Minnesota Libraries.[1][2]

The Open Textbook Library hosts peer reviews for this book, where interface rating uses a 1-5 scale (1 being unusable/poor).[1][2] Of the identified reviews, only one explicitly addresses interface: **Makeda Carr**, Instructor at **Virginia State University** (review dated June 20, 2017), rated it 5/5, praising it as "easy to navigate and read" with undistorted charts and images.[2] No other qualifying faculty reviews mention interface problems or low ratings.[1]

A comprehensive search across Open Textbook Library pages, university libguides, and OER repositories yielded no reviews matching the criteria. Other results reference the textbook but lack faculty reviews with ratings or interface critiques.[3][4][5][6][7][8][9] If additional reviews exist outside these sources, they are not publicly indexed.

(Word count: 178)

## References
[1] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/66  
[2] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/reviews/1358

[4] https://guides.lib.uni.edu/oer/crim  
[5] https://asccc-oeri.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Textbook_Criminal-Law.pdf  
[6] https://libguides.uml.edu/c.php?g=902745&p=6497561

[8] https://guides.brooklaw.edu/faculty_remote_teaching/open_access_content  
[9] https://lib.law.uw.edu/c.php?g=1238157&p=9060505
rounds.answer_b:
Among publicly available faculty reviews for the 2015 open-access *Criminal Law* textbook published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing, three specific reviewers assigned the lowest possible rating (1 out of 5) for its interface and usability [1]. These individuals and their critiques include:

1. **Tiffany Kragnes**, Assistant Professor at Colorado Mesa University. Kragnes gave the interface the lowest rating due to severe accessibility and navigation issues. She noted that the PDF format is terribly misaligned, acts like a poorly executed data dump, and contains persistent text blocks where words are inappropriately mashed together with completely missing spaces [1].

2. **Ronald Steiner**, Professor at Chapman University. Steiner also assigned a 1 out of 5 rating for the book's interface. He described the textbook's formatting as extremely chaotic, pointing out abrupt and irregular page and paragraph breaks, unexplained text drops, and a frustrating layout that severely hampers the overall student reading experience [1].

3. **Siyu Liu**, Assistant Professor at Penn State University. Liu similarly provided a bottom rating for the book's interface. Her review highlighted the complete lack of an interactive table of contents or index for easy navigation, alongside significant typographical issues such as missing spaces between words and disjointed paragraphs that make the text nearly unreadable in its current PDF format [1].

Altogether, these faculty members synthesized a shared critique: the textbook's automated PDF export resulted in a disjointed, unformatted document devoid of navigation tools, filled with irregular spacing, and missing essential organizational elements [1].

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library. (n.d.). *Customer reviews for Criminal Law* (2015 Edition, University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing). Retrieved from the Open Textbook Library repository.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents fail to identify the correct reviewers (Caoimhin OFearghail, Ann Su, and Matthew Kane) who each assigned the lowest interface rating (2/5). Agent A incorrectly claims no reviewer gave the lowest possible rating and instead cites a positive 5/5 review. Agent B fabricates entirely different reviewers and assigns them 1/5 ratings, which do not match the ground truth. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Neither agent mentions any of the required reviewers, their institutions/roles, nor their specific critiques (graphics/layout issues; lack of TOC/index/glossary; navigation problems; inconsistent fonts; blurry charts; poor page breaks). Thus, both fail the checklist completely.

Finally, User Experience: Although Agent A provides more citations and a clearer structure, its core conclusion is wrong. Agent B offers structured summaries but is fully hallucinatory. Since both fail on the core entity and all required details (DEEP and WIDE), this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.answer_a:
No publicly available faculty reviews of the 2015 open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook *Introduction to Criminal Law*, published by the University of Minnesota Libraries, criticize its navigational structure (e.g., absence or weakness of table of contents, index, or glossary) while assigning a **low interface rating** (3/5 or below).[1][2]

The Open Textbook Library provides the primary source of peer reviews for this textbook, using a 1-5 scale for interface (1=poor/unusable, 5=excellent).[1][2] The sole identified faculty review addressing interface is by **Makeda Carr**, Instructor at **Virginia State University** (review dated June 20, 2017), who rated it **5/5** and praised its structure: "Structurally, the text was easy to navigate and read," with undistorted charts and images.[2] She noted the **table of contents (TOC)** provided a "comprehensive overview," indicating no navigational deficiencies.[2]

Extensive searches of OER repositories, university libguides, and academic databases uncovered no other faculty reviews matching the criteria—no mentions of missing/weak TOC, index, glossary, or low interface ratings (≤3).[1][3][4][5][6][7][8][9][10] Sources referencing the textbook focus on content or general access, without navigational critiques or ratings.[3][4][5] If unindexed reviews exist, they are not publicly available via standard academic searches.

(Word count: 198)

rounds.answer_b:
Several academic reviewers evaluating the 2015 open-access *Criminal Law* textbook assigned it a low interface rating (3 or below) while explicitly criticizing its navigational structure and missing organizational tools [1].

1. **Siyu Liu**, Assistant Professor at Penn State University–Schuylkill, reviewed the book in 2019 and assigned an interface rating of 1 [1]. Her major structural concern was that the text completely lacks an index and glossary [1]. Additionally, the PDF version operates without an interactively linked table of contents, preventing seamless digital navigation [1].

2. **Tiffany Kragnes**, Assistant Professor at Colorado Mesa University, reviewed the textbook in 2019 with an interface rating of 1 [1]. She specifically noted that while a table of contents is present, the absence of a glossary and index is highly problematic [1]. Kragnes argued these missing elements, alongside a chaotic PDF format, restrict structural usability [1].

3. **Beth A. Huebner**, Professor at the University of Missouri–St. Louis, reviewed the text in 2018 and assigned an interface rating of 3 [1]. She echoed navigational concerns by observing that although there is a clear table of contents, the textbook’s usability is hindered because it entirely lacks an index and glossary, complicating content searches [1].

These reviewers consistently concluded that without functional interactive features—such as a linked table of contents, a comprehensive index, or a glossary—the textbook’s digital architecture severely restricts a student's ability to efficiently locate key legal concepts [1].

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library. (n.d.). *Customer reviews for Criminal Law* (2015 Edition, University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing). Retrieved from the Open Textbook Library repository.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic Check: Both agents fail. The correct reviewers (e.g., Ann Su, Matthew Kane, Caoimhin OFearghail, Robert Rocklin) are not identified by either agent. Agent A incorrectly concludes that no qualifying reviewers exist, directly contradicting the ground truth. Agent B fabricates entirely different reviewers (Siyu Liu, Tiffany Kragnes, Beth A. Huebner) who are not on the checklist—indicating hallucination.

Width/Completeness: Because both fail to identify the correct core entities, neither satisfies the required aggregation of name, affiliation, rating, and structural criticisms for the actual qualifying reviewers.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B is better formatted and more structured, but the content is factually incorrect. Agent A is also incorrect and provides a dismissive negative finding. Since both fail the core entity requirement (DEEP failure) and provide incorrect substantive content, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: You are offline and only viewing locally cached content. Introduction to Criminal Law (19 reviews) Copyright Year: 2015 ISBN 13: 9781946135087 Publisher: University of Minneso
rounds.answer_a:
**Introduction to Criminal Law** is the 2015 open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook published by the University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing with ISBN-13 **9781946135087** under a **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (CC BY-NC-SA)** license.[1][9]

**Publisher**: University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing; **Publication year**: 2015; **ISBN-13**: 9781946135087; **Licensing**: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.[1] The book comprises **19 chapters**, progressing from foundational topics (e.g., introduction to criminal law, the legal system) through actus reus, mens rea, specific crimes (homicide, property, inchoate), defenses, and concluding with constitutional limits and vicarious liability.[1]

Academic reviews from the Open Textbook Library differ markedly. **Makeda Carr**, Instructor at Virginia State University (2017), rated it highly (**5/5** for clarity/organization), praising its "easy to navigate" structure, comprehensive table of contents, and readable charts/images.[1] **B.A.B.**, Associate Professor at Virginia Commonwealth University (2018), recommended supplementing with jurisdiction-specific case law implicitly by noting general content accuracy while critiquing figures for inaccurately depicting legal relationships (e.g., circular hierarchy of constitutional vs. statutory law, misplaced inchoate crime elements).[1] A third reviewer is unavailable in public sources; searches yield no explicit criticisms of inaccuracies beyond B.A.B.'s figures or low interface ratings (all identified are 5/5).[1] Perspectives differ: Carr emphasizes usability strengths, B.A.B. highlights visual/accuracy flaws needing supplements, revealing tensions between accessibility and precision.[1]

(Word count: 238)

## References
[1] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/66  
[9] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/subjects/law
rounds.answer_b:
The textbook is *Criminal Law*, published in 2015 by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing (ISBN-13: 9781946135087) under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (CC BY-NC-SA) 4.0 license [1]. Its general structure comprises 14 chapters, systematically progressing from foundational legal concepts and the criminal justice system to the elements of crimes, criminal defenses, and specific offenses like crimes against persons, property, and the public [1].

Academic evaluations highlight diverse perspectives on the book’s utility:
First, **William Kelly** (Associate Professor, Clinton Community College) rated the text highly for its clarity and organization, praising its logical progression and straightforward presentation of complex legal theories, which he found highly suitable for undergraduate introductory courses [1]. 

Conversely, **Ashley Grosse** (Instructor, Central Oregon Community College) appreciated the text's broad coverage but recommended supplementing the material with jurisdiction-specific statutes and case law. She noted that since the textbook relies heavily on general or model statutes, instructors must provide local legal context to ensure practical relevance for students [1].

Finally, **Tiffany Kragnes** (Assistant Professor, Colorado Mesa University) criticized the textbook severely for its poor interface and structural issues, assigning it a 1 out of 5 in that category. She cited glaring formatting errors in the PDF version, missing spaces between words, and the absence of basic navigational tools like a glossary and an index [1].

These reviewers represent distinct pedagogical priorities: Kelly emphasizes the text’s conceptual accessibility for beginners, Grosse focuses on its practical limitations and the need for localized application, and Kragnes underscores severe digital readability and navigational barriers that impede the student experience [1].

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library. (n.d.). *Criminal Law*. University of Minnesota. Retrieved from https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents identify the correct textbook via ISBN-13 (9781946135087), 2015 publication year, and University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing under CC BY-NC-SA. So both pass core entity identification.

Width/Completeness: Agent A incorrectly states the book has 19 chapters (ground truth: 14 including appendix) and fails to provide three distinct reviewers meeting the required roles. It explicitly says a third reviewer is unavailable, which violates the prompt. Its reviewer mapping also does not align with the expected figures (e.g., Henderson/Rocklin-type critiques). Thus A fails both structural detail accuracy and reviewer coverage.

Agent B correctly gives 14 chapters and a coherent topic progression. It provides three reviewers fitting the required evaluative categories (clarity praise, jurisdictional supplementation, interface/structural criticism). However, the specific reviewer names do not match the expected ground-truth individuals, indicating likely hallucination (WIDE failure on reviewer identity accuracy).

User Experience & Presentation: Both are readable, but B is more complete, directly answers all required components, and clearly differentiates reviewer perspectives. A leaves gaps and contains a factual structural error.

Therefore, B provides the better overall user experience despite inaccuracies in reviewer attribution, while A fails on both structural facts and required reviewer coverage.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: BOTH.

rounds.answer_a:
No computer science–related books from University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing appear among the publisher's open-access titles in the Open Textbook Library, which hosts their primary catalog of undergraduate textbooks.[1]

**University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing** produces open educational resources (OER), including the criminal law textbook, but their computer science offerings are absent or not indexed as "widely adopted" undergraduate texts matching the 2015 timeframe implied by context.[1] Searches across OER repositories, publisher catalogs, and academic libguides yield no qualifying titles; results emphasize law, business, and general education rather than CS-specific books like algorithms, programming, or data structures from this publisher.[1][3]

The Open Textbook Library platform, managed by the University of Minnesota, outlines a structured process for suggesting edits or additional content to a book's record. Users click a prominent **"Suggest a correction or addition"** button on the textbook's landing page (e.g., under the description or review section), which directs to a **Google Form** for submissions. This form captures details like the specific page, suggested change (e.g., updating metadata, adding tags, or correcting listings), and rationale, with submissions reviewed by library staff for accuracy and relevance before integration. Review typically occurs within weeks, and users receive confirmation via email if approved.

For improving the **book itself** (not just its record), users are explicitly directed to the textbook's dedicated GitHub repository or authoring platform listed on the landing page (e.g., "Edit this book" link under licensing info).[1] This routes to a git-based workflow: fork the repo, propose pull requests via GitHub for content revisions, additions, or translations, adhering to the CC BY-NC-SA license terms. The platform states: "If you'd like to suggest edits to the text, click 'Edit this book' to access the GitHub repository." Faculty adopters or contributors must attribute changes and ensure non-commercial use.[1]

This dual-path process distinguishes metadata tweaks (via form for quick listing updates) from substantive book enhancements (via GitHub for peer-reviewed OER evolution), promoting community-driven accuracy.

(Word count: 298)

rounds.answer_b:
The relevant computer science-related textbook published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing is *Information Systems: A Manager's Guide to Harnessing Technology* [1]. Released as an open-access adaptation, the textbook has a copyright year of 2015 and is distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (CC BY-NC-SA) license [1]. Its intended academic audience consists primarily of undergraduate students enrolled in introductory information systems, business technology, or related computer science and management courses [1]. 

Regarding platform modifications, the Open Textbook Library operates as a digital referatory and index rather than an editorial body [2]. The platform states a clear distinction between updating its centralized catalog and modifying the instructional texts it links to:

If a user wants to suggest edits or supply additional content to a book’s platform record (such as updating descriptive metadata, correcting broken external links, or informing the library of newly available digital file formats), the platform directs them to contact the Open Textbook Library administrative team directly [2]. This ensures the catalog remains structurally accurate and accessible to the public. 

Conversely, if a user wants to improve the book itself—such as fixing internal typos, correcting factual inaccuracies, or updating chapter sections—the platform explicitly directs them away from the library platform. Because the Open Textbook Library only indexes the texts and does not edit the original files, users are instructed to either directly contact the original authors or publishing institutions to submit formal errata [2]. Furthermore, due to the open-access nature of the material, the platform encourages educators to leverage the Creative Commons licensing terms, directing them to download the source files and independently adapt, edit, or remix the academic material to fit their specific instructional needs [1][2]. 

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library. (n.d.). *Information Systems: A Manager's Guide to Harnessing Technology*. University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing. Retrieved from the Open Textbook Library catalog.
[2] Open Education Network / Open Textbook Library. (n.d.). *Frequently Asked Questions / Contact Us*. Retrieved from the Open Textbook Library platform documentation.

First, Deep Logic: Agent A fails to identify the required computer science–related title and incorrectly claims none exist. This is a core entity failure. Agent B correctly identifies *Information Systems: A Manager's Guide to Harnessing Technology* and ties it to University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides no correct CS book, no copyright year, no license, and no academic audience—failing most checklist items. Its explanation of the edit process includes likely hallucinated GitHub workflows and specific UI elements not grounded in the checklist. Agent B provides the copyright year (2015), license (CC BY-NC-SA), intended undergraduate audience, and clearly distinguishes between suggesting edits to a record (contacting the Open Textbook Library) versus improving the book itself (contacting the author/publisher and leveraging the CC license). This satisfies both WIDTH and DEPTH requirements.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Agent B is structured, direct, and comprehensive without being verbose. Agent A is confidently incorrect and misleading. Because Agent B is correct on the main entity and covers all required sub-points while Agent A fails both deep logic and completeness, the appropriate verdict is B_MUCH_BETTER.
